YP23eR0AAAAJ.keywords: SAT/SMT Solvers, AI, software engineering, mathematical logic, quantum foundations, Symbolic execution, String constraint solving, Fuzzing and concolic testing, Bit-vector and array decision procedures, SAT heuristics and machine learning
YP23eR0AAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Vijay Ganesh is a **Professor** in the School of Computer Science at the **Georgia Institute of Technology** in Atlanta, where he leads a research group focused on advancing automated reasoning, program analysis, and the practical application of formal methods. His lab develops high-impact tools and algorithms at the intersection of *software engineering*, *mathematical logic*, and *artificial intelligence*, with particular emphasis on <u>symbolic execution</u>, <u>SMT/SAT solving</u>, and <u>string constraint solving</u>. Ganesh’s work spans both foundational decision procedures and pragmatic bug-finding systems, producing widely used artifacts (e.g., STP, EXE, HAMPI and several string-solving extensions for Z3) and bridging theory and practice in automated analysis. His body of work has accumulated over **9,700 citations**, reflecting broad influence across programming languages, security, verification, and solver engineering.

---

## Research Areas
Ganesh’s research advances automated reasoning techniques and their application to real-world software and security problems. He has made foundational contributions to the design and implementation of decision procedures for low-level program models—most notably efficient solvers for the theory of bit-vectors and arrays (STP) and specialized string/regex/length solvers (HAMPI, Z3str2/3/4). He has pioneered and popularized practical *symbolic-execution–based* bug-finding and concolic-fuzzing systems (EXE, BuzzFuzz, jFuzz), demonstrating how <u>symbolic execution</u> and <u>taint-based directed fuzzing</u> can systematically generate crash- and vulnerability-triggering inputs. In parallel, Ganesh has explored solver heuristics and ML-driven solver selection—introducing the view of branching as an optimization problem (learning-rate and LRB heuristics), ML-based restart policies, and algorithm-selection frameworks for SMT (MachSMT). His recent work applies machine learning and explainability techniques to solver and model behavior, and extends automated-reasoning methods into domains as diverse as smart-contract analysis, cryptanalysis, combinatorial search, and even aspects of quantum foundations and interpretable physics models.

---

## Notable Works
- EXE: Automatically generating inputs of death (2008) — Introduced a practical symbolic-execution based bug-finder that systematically generates crashing inputs for real-world programs, catalyzing subsequent work in concolic testing and whitebox fuzzing.  
- A decision procedure for bit-vectors and arrays (STP) (2007) — Presented the STP solver optimized for large-scale software-analysis problems, establishing a high-performance word-level decision procedure widely used in verification and security.  
- HAMPI / Z3-based string solvers and solver heuristics (HAMPI 2009; Z3str3 2017; Learning-rate branching heuristic 2016) — Developed specialized string constraint solvers and theory-aware heuristics, and introduced ML-informed branching strategies that materially improved SAT/SMT solver performance on industry benchmarks.

---

## Academic Background
Vijay Ganesh is a senior academic with a long record of tool-building, publications, and community leadership in formal methods and software-reliability research. As a faculty member at Georgia Tech he has supervised numerous students and collaborators, maintained an active public repository of tools and resources (homepage: https://vganesh1.github.io/), and contributed to cross-disciplinary projects spanning academia and industry. His work has been recognized by extensive citation impact, repeated adoption of his tools in verification and security pipelines, and sustained involvement in program committees and community standards (e.g., SMT-LIB and solver benchmarking efforts). Ganesh’s publication portfolio shows continuous contributions from systems and architecture description languages in the late 1990s to contemporary advances in ML-augmented solvers and automated program analysis, reflecting a career that blends theoretical depth with practical engineering and broad collaborative engagement.

Yg_QjxcAAAAJ.keywords: Artificial intelligence, Machine Learning, Storytelling, Explainable AI, Safe AI, Narrative planning, Computational narrative, Interactive narrative, Automated story generation, Procedural content generation
Yg_QjxcAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Mark Riedl is a **Professor of Computing** at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads an active research group focused on the intersection of **artificial intelligence**, **machine learning**, and narrative technologies (see http://www.cc.gatech.edu/~riedl/). His work centers on enabling machines to *create, understand, and communicate stories* and on making AI systems more transparent, trustworthy, and aligned with human values. Central to his agenda are the <u>computational modeling of narrative</u>, <u>interactive narrative and game-centered AI</u>, and <u>human-centered explainable AI (XAI)</u>—investigations that span from symbolic planning approaches for plot and character to data-driven neural methods and socio-technical analyses of explainability.

---

## Research Areas
Riedl’s research bridges several interrelated areas in AI and HCI. In narrative intelligence and interactive storytelling he develops systems for <u>narrative planning</u>, authorable interactive narratives, and multi-agent story generation (e.g., work on balancing plot and character, plan-driven behavior for game environments, and transitioning from linear story generation to branching story graphs). In creative and procedural content generation he explores story realization, plot-graph based crowd-sourced story generation, and procedural game-world and level generation from data (including gameplay video and co-creative level design). In explainability and human-centered AI he advances a socio-technical view of XAI—introducing concepts such as *human-centered explainable AI (HCXAI)*, *social transparency*, and automated rationale/rationalization techniques that translate agent internals into natural-language explanations. Riedl also investigates value alignment and safe-AI themes, using stories and narratives as mechanisms for conveying values to agents and for studying downstream effects and pitfalls of explanations in real sociotechnical settings.

---

## Notable Works
- "Narrative planning: Balancing plot and character" (2010) — seminal work establishing planning-based methods for generating stories that reconcile global plot coherence with believable character behavior, foundational to subsequent interactive narrative and story-generation research.  
- "Expanding explainability: Towards social transparency in ai systems" (2021) — advances a socio-organizational perspective on explainability, arguing that explanations must account for the social context in which AI systems operate and proposing directions for socially aware XAI design.  
- "Automated rationale generation: a technique for explainable AI and its effects on human perceptions" (2019) — introduces and evaluates a method for producing human-like natural language rationales from agent state/action data, empirically studying how such rationales shape user perceptions of autonomous systems.

---

## Academic Background
Riedl’s publication record traces back to the early 2000s with a sustained focus on interactive narrative, game AI, and later human-centered explainability. As a senior faculty member at Georgia Tech, he operates at the nexus of the College of Computing, game research, and HCI communities; his collaborations include co-authors and contributors from academic (e.g., Cornell, IBM Research) and industry partners visible in multi-author projects. His body of work has been widely cited (over **15,600** total citations), reflecting influence across AI, machine learning, human-computer interaction, and game research. Riedl’s portfolio combines theory, systems-building, empirical human-subject studies, and socio-technical critique, and he regularly contributes to conferences and journals in AI, HCI, and computational creativity.
